perturbation.original_text: Each Party, to the extent of its right to do so, shall disclose to the other Party the Proprietary Information which are a ppropriate to fulfil the objectives of this Agreement as set up in the recitals. The Parties hereby represent that the disclosure of Propri etary Information by and between themselves is not contrary to the laws and regulations of their respective countries.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party, to the extent of its right to do so, may disclose to the other Party the Proprietary Information which are a ppropriate to fulfil the objectives of this Agreement as set up in the recitals. The Parties hereby represent that the disclosure of Propri etary Information by and between themselves is not contrary to the laws and regulations of their respective countries.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall disclose' to 'may disclose' introduces a contradiction. The original wording mandates the disclosure of appropriate proprietary information, while the modified version makes it optional. This contradicts the overall purpose of the NDA, which is to protect disclosed information, implying an obligation to disclose relevant information for the collaboration. This change will contradict with section 4, as section 4 it gives the receiving party the convents, which they wouldn't be able to perform without the other party having an obligation to disclose the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safegu ards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care, (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement. (Buyer shall be entitled to freely disclose such information to its affiliated companies, to its co -contractors for a project and to its final customer). (c) not be used in whole or in part for any purpose other than the purpose of this Agreement as specified in the preamble and for the Project above without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party, (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project, (e) neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplic ation have not been specifically authorized in writing by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall disclose' to 'may disclose' introduces a contradiction because it changes a mandatory obligation to a discretionary option. The original text obligated each party to disclose proprietary information, while the changed text makes it optional. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which outlines the receiving party's responsibilities regarding 'the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party.' If the disclosing party 'may' disclose, but is not obligated to, there is no guarantee the receiving party will ever 'receive' any Proprietary Information, rendering the covenants in the contradicted text potentially unenforceable or meaningless. This fits the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' since altering the 'shall' obligation to 'may' creates a contradiction with the obligations in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safegu ards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care, (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement. (Buyer shall be entitled to freely disclose such information to its affiliated companies, to its co -contractors for a project and to its final customer). (c) not be used in whole or in part for any purpose other than the purpose of this Agreement as specified in the preamble and for the Project above without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party, (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project, (e) neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplic ation have not been specifically authorized in writing by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and until the end of the project, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safegu ards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care, (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement. (Buyer shall be entitled to freely disclose such information to its affiliated companies, to its co -contractors for a project and to its final customer). (c) not be used in whole or in part for any purpose other than the purpose of this Agreement as specified in the preamble and for the Project above without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party, (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project, (e) neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplic ation have not been specifically authorized in writing by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the duration of confidentiality obligations from 'five (5) years after its end or its termination' to 'until the end of the project' creates a contradiction with Section 11. Section 11 explicitly states that the obligations imposed by paragraph 4 continue for the period applicable as set forth in said paragraph, which was originally five years. Now, with the change in Section 4, it becomes unclear whether Section 11 still refers to a five-year period or the end of the project, leading to ambiguity in the enforcement of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The end or termination of this Agreement shall not relieve the receiving Party of complying with the obligations imposed by paragraph 4 thereof with respect to the use and protection of the Proprietary Information received prior to the date of termi nation or end of this Agreement. Such obligations shall continue for the period applicable as set forth in said paragraph.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that confidentiality obligations lasted for five years after termination. The changed text modifies this to last only until the end of the project. Section 11 states that obligations imposed by paragraph 4 continue for the period applicable as set forth in said paragraph, which was originally five years. Now, with the change in Section 4, it becomes unclear whether Section 11 still refers to a five-year period or the end of the project, leading to ambiguity in the enforcement of confidentiality and resulting in an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any Proprietary Information and copies thereof disclosed by either Party to the other shall remain the property of the dis closing Party and shall be returned by the receiving Party immediately upon request. The above provisions shall not apply to the Proprietary Information disclosed by the Supplier to the Buyer and which fall und er clause 12.2 of the General Purchase Conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: Any Proprietary Information and copies thereof disclosed by either Party to the other shall remain the property of the dis closing Party and shall be destroyed by the receiving Party immediately upon request. The above provisions shall not apply to the Proprietary Information disclosed by the Supplier to the Buyer and which fall und er clause 12.2 of the General Purchase Conditions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'returned' to 'destroyed' creates a direct conflict within section 5 itself and potential contradictions with other sections. The modification now requires the receiving party to destroy the Proprietary Information upon request, which contradicts the potential need to 'return' it if destruction is not feasible or if the disclosing party prefers its return for certain reasons. This directly contradicts with section 8 which gives the buyer the rights to their data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Except as provided under clause 12.2 of the General Purchase Conditions, it is expressly understood and agreed by the Par ties hereto that the disclosure and provision of Proprietary Information under this Agreement by a Party to the other Party shall notbe construed as granting to the receiving Party under the laws of any country any rights whether expressed or implied by licence or otherwise on the matters, inventions or discoveries to which such Proprietary Information pertains or any copyright, trademar k or trade secret rights. The property in all information and/or data disclosed by the Buyer to the Supplier pursuant to this Agreement shall subject t o any right of any other owner, rest with the Buyer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text by replacing 'returned' with 'destroyed' regarding Proprietary Information. The explanation states this change creates a direct conflict within section 5 and potential contradictions with other sections, particularly concerning the requirement to 'return' information if destruction is not feasible or preferred by the disclosing party. The contradicted text from section 8 grants the buyer rights to their data. The change from 'returned' to 'destroyed' now creates an incompatibility, as the buyer's right to data implies it may need to be returned, but the new clause mandates destruction, leading to a strong in-text contradiction as per the explanation and definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall enter into force on the date of its signature and shall be valid for a term of 5(five) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall enter into force on the date of its signature and shall be valid for a term of 5(five) years, unless terminated earlier by either party with a 30-day written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'unless terminated earlier by either party with a 30-day written notice' introduces a contradiction with the original fixed term of 5 years stated. While the original clause suggests a binding agreement for a specific duration, the modified clause allows premature termination, which creates uncertainty regarding the actual duration of the agreement and conflicts with Section 11, which outlines obligations that extend beyond the termination date, implying a certain level of continuity beyond simple termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The end or termination of this Agreement shall not relieve the receiving Party of complying with the obligations imposed by paragraph 4 thereof with respect to the use and protection of the Proprietary Information received prior to the date of termi nation or end of this Agreement. Such obligations shall continue for the period applicable as set forth in said paragraph.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a fixed term of 5 years. The changed text adds a termination clause, allowing either party to terminate with 30 days' notice. This directly contradicts the fixed term as it introduces the possibility of the agreement ending before the 5-year period. This creates uncertainty about the agreement's actual duration. The contradicted text, regarding obligations extending beyond termination, assumes a certain level of continuity or at least a predictable termination based on the original 5-year term. The added termination clause undermines this assumption, leading to a contradiction. The change in text falls under the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' as it makes the agreement duration ambiguous and inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: This Contract shall be governed by Buyer country laws and its appropriate courts of Paris. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the competent jurisdiction of Paris in France shall be the sole competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Contract shall be governed by the laws of the Supplier's country, unless otherwise mutually agreed upon in writing. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the parties will first attempt to resolve it through mediation before resorting to legal action.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a significant contradiction regarding governing law and dispute resolution. The original clause specifies that Buyer's country laws and the courts of Paris have jurisdiction. The modified clause suggests Supplier's country laws, potentially, and mandates mediation before legal action. This contradicts the original clause by changing the legal framework and enforcement mechanism, and will affect section 12 as in the original text states it is governed by Buyers country laws and the appropriate courts of paris.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Contract shall be governed by Buyer country laws and its appropriate courts of Paris. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the competent jurisdiction of Paris in France shall be the sole competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction to the contradicted text. The original text states that the contract is governed by the Buyer's country laws and courts in Paris. The changed text proposes that the Supplier's country laws govern the contract, 'unless otherwise mutually agreed upon,' and mandates mediation before legal action. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which specifies the Buyer's country's laws and the jurisdiction of Paris courts, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.
